message:
fix(jsonl): single-write_all per line for concurrent O_APPEND; drop NaN heartbeats

Three independent call sites append JSONL records to shared history files
from concurrent tasks, and all three split the record into two writes
(JSON body, then `\n`). POSIX `O_APPEND` only serializes at the
single-syscall boundary, so two writers racing produced interleaved lines
of the form `{record_a}{record_b}\n\n` instead of
`{record_a}\n{record_b}\n`. That corrupts the JSONL stream — history
replay on the next daemon start sees an unparseable line and either
drops records or halts recovery.

Fix is the same in all three places: build a single buffer containing
`json_bytes + "\n"` and hand it to one `write_all`. For the <PIPE_BUF-
sized payloads we write (build records, self-test entries, fleet
history), the glibc/Linux path collapses this to a single `write()`
syscall that O_APPEND does serialize.

  rch/src/fleet/history.rs
    HistoryManager::append_entry: sync std::fs::File path. Fleet
    deployments run workers in parallel via tokio::spawn, so
    concurrent appends are normal here.

  rchd/src/history.rs
    BuildHistory::persist_record_async: tokio AsyncFile path. Every
    completed build spawns its own task calling this; this is the
    hottest JSONL writer in the daemon.

  rchd/src/self_test.rs
    persist_entry_async: same pattern for self-test history.

Also piggy-backs a defensive drop on the heartbeat `progress_percent`
handler in `rchd/src/history.rs`:

  `f64::clamp` returns NaN when given NaN — so a worker sending a `NaN`
  progress percent would poison the heartbeat state. Every subsequent
  `percent` comparison yielded `false` (NaN never compares >), leaving
  the status display stuck at "NaN%" for the rest of the build.
  Guarded by `.is_finite()`: a bogus percent is silently ignored; a
  progress bar with no reading is strictly better than a frozen one.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
